USD.catalyst: The U.S. Fed 3.75%. While soft employment data initially triggered the greenback's largest weekly drop in three months, a recent uptick in U.S. bond yields and geopolitical tensions in the Strait of Hormuz have provided a floor for the currency. Despite the cooling labor market, a 150bp carry advantage over the Euro and hawkish Fed pricing continue to support the dollar's broader structural bid. Traders are currently monitoring the rebound in AI-related equities, which has bolstered risk sentiment and stabilized the dollar against the South Korean won and other emerging market peers.

sources.title: Dollar heads for weekly drop as jobs data dims Fed hike bets - CNBC
US stocks open higher as gains for AI-related stocks support the market - The Sun Chronicle
Dollar near two-week lows as rate-hike bets recede, embattled yen in focus - CNBC
US stocks tick higher after AI winners rebound - The Sun Chronicle

GBP.catalyst: Sterling is maintaining a firm tone near $1.3395, supported by a fading of domestic political risk and a subsequent unwind of short positions. Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey recently noted that domestic inflation would likely be at target if not for the impact of external shocks, while policymaker Catherine Mann’s recent commentary has kept markets focused on the BoE's restrictive stance. Despite the spot strength, the options market remains cautious, with the 25-delta risk reversal showing a -0.67 put skew, indicating a lingering bias for GBP weakness. The currency continues to track broader risk sentiment, recently buoyed by weekly gains in the FTSE 100 financials and mining sectors.

sources.title: London's FTSE indexes post weekly gain as financials, miners advance - KITCO

EUR.catalyst: The Euro is hovering near a two-week peak at $1.1446, benefiting from the broad-based softening of the U.S. dollar following weak American employment data. However, ECB Governing Council member Fabio Panetta recently signaled a cautious approach, stating that a fragile economic outlook means there is no predetermined path for interest rates while highlighting that upside inflation risks coexist with downside growth risks. The single currency remains constrained by a significant 150bp interest rate disadvantage against the Fed's 3.75% policy rate, leaving it vulnerable to carry-driven outflows. Market participants are also weighing Panetta’s comments regarding uncertainty over governance in the Strait of Hormuz as a potential headwind for Eurozone trade stability.

sources.title: Yen Hits Fresh 40-Year Low Versus Dollar; Traders Alert for Possible FX Intervention - marketscreener.com

JPY.catalyst: The Japanese yen remains pinned near 40-year lows, with USD/JPY trading around 161.89 as a massive 275bp Fed-BoJ carry differential sustains a crowded short position of -137,828 contracts among leveraged funds. Goldman Sachs has revised its 12-month USD/JPY forecast to 165, citing Japan’s debt crisis—which has reached 240% of GDP—and suggesting that any Ministry of Finance intervention would only provide temporary relief. While softer-than-expected wage data has complicated the outlook, markets are still pricing the possibility of the Bank of Japan raising rates to a 31-year high at the July 31 meeting to counter oil shocks. Traders remain on high alert for official Tokyo intervention as the pair tests the critical 162.00 level.

sources.title: Yen to extend historic slump, while AI and energy 'supply bust' to support the U.S. dollar: Goldman - CNBC
Yen crashes as Japan’s debt crisis hits currency markets, and interventions are 'doomed to fail' - Fortune
Stocks ease despite upbeat Samsung forecast, yen languishes - Reuters
Yen Hits Fresh 40-Year Low Versus Dollar; Traders Alert for Possible FX Intervention - marketscreener.com

AUD.catalyst: The Australian dollar is testing two-week highs near 0.6956, supported by a net long positioning of +21,597 contracts among leveraged funds and significant domestic M&A activity, including Genesis Minerals' AUD 5.6 billion bid for Vault Minerals. While the currency has benefited from higher gold prices and a $65.7 million economic boost from UFC 325 in Sydney, elevated implied volatility of 38.1% suggests the market is heavily hedging against downside risks. Technical resistance is noted near 0.6980, with the 38.2% Fibonacci level at 0.6955 serving as a key pivot point. Market participants are now looking ahead to the Reserve Bank of Australia’s policy meeting on August 11 for the next major directional catalyst.

sources.title: Mining company Genesis bids 5.6 billion Australian dollars to acquire gold miner Vault - Bitget
UFC Delivers Knockout Results For Sydney - UFC.com

CAD.catalyst: The Canadian dollar reached a 10-day high of 1.4147 against the U.S. dollar following soft U.S. jobs data, though it remains pressured by long-term uncertainty surrounding the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) renewal. BoC 2.25%. The currency is currently caught between the supportive influence of higher oil prices and the structural drag of a "crowded support" for the USD into year-end. Market focus remains on the impending USMCA deadline, which has historically triggered steep monthly declines for the CAD.

sources.title: Analysts cut Canadian dollar forecasts as USMCA uncertainty clips rate hike chances - KITCO
Canadian dollar hits 10-day high as US job growth slows - KITCO
Canadian dollar heads for steep monthly decline ahead of USMCA deadline - KITCO

CHF.catalyst: The Swiss franc is currently finding buyers on dips, though it has recently struggled to maintain its safe-haven dominance against a resilient U.S. dollar. USD/CHF is testing resistance at 0.8075, rebounding from support levels established at the end of last week as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East drive flows toward the greenback. While the franc has seen some relief from the unwind of sterling shorts in the GBP/CHF cross, the domestic policy outlook remains overshadowed by the broader G10 carry trade environment. Traders are monitoring the 0.8075 level as a primary indicator of whether the franc can regain its footing as a preferred defensive asset.

NZD.catalyst: The New Zealand dollar is trading near 0.5703, with market attention fixed on the Reserve Bank of New Zealand’s policy meeting this Wednesday, where Westpac expects the official cash rate to remain unchanged at 2.25%. Despite a crowded speculative net short position of -25,757 contracts, the currency remains subdued due to falling oil prices and renewed tensions in the Strait of Hormuz. There is a significant short-squeeze risk if the RBNZ delivers a hawkish surprise or signals a hike for the July 27 meeting, with potential targets for an unwind extending toward 0.5900. However, Commerzbank analysts warn that the path for the Kiwi remains questioned even if a hike is delivered, given the broader strength of the USD.

sources.title: Westpac: Reserve Bank of New Zealand has time to wait for more data - Bitget

NOK.catalyst: The Norwegian krone remains largely range-bound against the major currencies as it lacks specific high-impact domestic catalysts in the current session. The currency continues to track global crude oil price fluctuations and broader European sentiment, with investors awaiting fresh inflation data or Norges Bank commentary to break the current consolidation. Without specific local data surprises or policy shifts, the krone is expected to remain sensitive to shifts in the Eurozone economic outlook and global risk appetite.

SEK.catalyst: The Swedish krona is currently trading within established ranges, pending fresh catalysts from the Riksbank or domestic economic releases. In the absence of specific named official commentary or policy decisions today, the currency is following broader G10 trends and Euro-area price action. Market participants are maintaining a neutral stance on the krona as it awaits a clearer signal regarding the relative pace of Swedish disinflation compared to its European peers.
